elements.sent-2411-009: Introduction In the United States, the foster care system is responsible for caring for children found to be victims of abuse or neglect at the hands of their parents.
elements.sent-2411-010: In 2022, there were 368,530 children in the foster care system (\qcitenp{afcars ).}
elements.sent-2411-011: Economists have evaluated numerous policy interventions to improve outcomes in foster care (\qcitenp{bald-2022 ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, one critical aspect of foster care has gone unnoticed: how should a child be matched to a temporary foster home? Existing processes for matching children to foster home are highly varied across U.S.
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, we cannot offer a uniform characterization of a child's journey from her home of origin to a foster home.
elements.sent-2411-014: Instead, we describe a broad generalization.
elements.sent-2411-015: Children that are suspected to be victims of abuse or neglect are often reported to the local child welfare agency by community members.
elements.sent-2411-016: The child welfare agency sends an investigator to the child's home, and, if the investigator substantiates the allegations, then the child is removed from her home.
elements.sent-2411-017: The agency assigns a caseworker to represent the child, and the child can be kept in custody of the county for up to several days before she is matched to a temporary foster home.
elements.sent-2411-018: The caseworker can use various means to search for a suitable match for the child, and this step in the process is the most heterogeneous step across counties.
elements.sent-2411-019: In most counties, caseworkers sequentially decide to match the child that the caseworker represents to the caseworker's most preferred available home, where the order is simply the first (or next) caseworker to initiate matching.
elements.sent-2411-020: We describe this time-ordered serial dictatorship as the "status quo".
elements.sent-2411-021: One main goal in the matching process is placement stability (\qcitenp{carnochan-2013 ).}
elements.sent-2411-022: A child's placement in a foster home is stable if the child remains in the home until the child is reunified with their home of origin, the foster home permanently adopts the child, or the child ages out of the foster care system.
elements.sent-2411-023: We refer to this as persistence to avoid confusion with the matching theory concept of stability.
elements.sent-2411-024: Persistence is not common\footnote{The average number of placements for a child was 2.56 in 2015; see \qcite{cortes-2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we develop matching mechanisms that are appropriate for this child welfare domain, and we do so while navigating a key sensitivity---caseworker approval.
elements.sent-2411-026: Caseworkers representing children have preferences over the homes that a child can be matched to.
elements.sent-2411-027: Often, these preferences can legitimately be in the best interests of the child and encode important, qualitative information that cannot be easily subsumed into algorithms that predict a child's persistence in a home.
elements.sent-2411-028: On the contrary, some caseworkers might have erroneous judgment or bias causing them to systemically choose homes where children will not persist.
elements.sent-2411-029: Nevertheless, the human element in the matching process is essential, and most counties are unwilling---justifiably so---to fully replace caseworkers with algorithms that decide placements.
elements.sent-2411-030: Our largest contribution is to design matching mechanisms that maintain caseworker approval---the status quo---in that every matching that our mechanism produces is the outcome of some serial dictatorship applied to the matching problem.
elements.sent-2411-031: Simultaneously, our mechanism ensures that no child could potentially improve her persistence without harming another child's persistence or some caseworker's preferences.
elements.sent-2411-032: We model this problem as a standard one-sided matching market where children are strategic agents allocated to homes.
elements.sent-2411-033: Children have ordinal preferences over homes that can involve indifference as there can be substantial horizontal differentiation that precludes constructing strict rankings in foster care\footnote{One can think of caseworkers analogously as the parents in school choice.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Parents, or caseworkers, actually have preferences over the objects, but we primarily refer to the students or children in exposition.. .
elements.sent-2411-035: We restrict the preference domain to most-least preferences with, at most, three strict classes (most preferred homes, less preferred homes, and unacceptable homes) which is necessary for tractability and applicable for our setting.
elements.sent-2411-036: We also allow the matchmaker to have strict ordinal preferences over homes that a given child can be placed in-and call these preferred outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-037: We require a familiar efficiency standard from our mechanism: weak Pareto efficiency, where in any allocation $\mu'$ that weakly Pareto dominates $\mu$, it must be that every child with a different home between the two matchings strictly prefers $\mu'$.
elements.sent-2411-038: This suffices to describe the efficiency of placements under the status quo.
elements.sent-2411-039: Furthermore, we impose a constrained welfare maximizing property that our mechanism must satisfy called outcome sufficiency.
elements.sent-2411-040: A matching $\mu$ is outcome dominated if there is another matching $\mu'$ where some subset of children improve their outcomes without making any other children worse off.
elements.sent-2411-041: A matching $\mu$ is outcome sufficient if it weakly Pareto efficient and not outcome dominated by any $\mu'$ that it does not Pareto dominate.
elements.sent-2411-042: We prove that our main mechanism, Linear Exchange, is outcome sufficient (Theorem \ref{theorem:linear_exchange_positive ).}
elements.sent-2411-043: Any matching that outcome dominates a matching that it produces must infringe on some caseworker's preferences, and any matching that Pareto dominates it must worsen some child's outcome.
elements.sent-2411-044: Turning to strategic incentives, we show that no outcome sufficient mechanism can be strategy proof (Theorem \ref{theorem:no-strategy-proof ).}
elements.sent-2411-045: This important result generalizes to any setting with Pareto-efficient constrained welfare maximization since outcome sufficiency is a weaker property than, for example, maximizing a sum of match valuations.
elements.sent-2411-046: However, this implies that Linear Exchange is manipulable (Proposition \ref{proposition:linear_exchange_manipulable ).}
elements.sent-2411-047: Furthermore, the manipulations are often simple truncation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-048: Nevertheless, we restore some positive results regarding the strategic incentives of Linear Exchange.
elements.sent-2411-049: We prove that it is non-obviously manipulable (Theorem \ref{theorem:linear_exchange_nom ) defined as in \qcite{troyan-2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Any child that successfully manipulates Linear Exchange needs both information that is not revealed to her and a favorable realization of uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-051: In our work in the future, we plan to conduct a lab-in-the-field experiment with real caseworkers in partnership with one of the largest child welfare organizations in the United States facilitating interdisciplinary research that improves practice.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our experiment will illicit caseworker preferences in a simulated market.
elements.sent-2411-053: Using a predicted persistence measure constructed from and validated on youth in foster care in a real United States county, we will compute the counterfactual matching and improvements in persistence in the simulated market over the status quo.
elements.sent-2411-054: This step to evaluate our mechanism in a plausible field environment is important to establish the existence (or non-existence) of misalignment in caseworker preferences and predicted persistence.
elements.sent-2411-055: Currently, we use fully simulated data and preferences to show the potential improvements in persistence.
elements.sent-2411-056: Literature Contribution---This paper intersects results on Pareto-constrained welfare maximization in computer science literature and standard matching theory.
elements.sent-2411-057: The work closest to ours is \qcite{biro-2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: They study the computational complexity of finding a Pareto efficient matching of highest welfare, where welfare in their case is a sum of match valuations and, in a large part of their work, where the match valuation depends only on one side.
elements.sent-2411-059: They find that this problem is NP-hard in general and prove stringent conditions under which it is computationally feasible.
elements.sent-2411-060: Following this, they define a general linear programming solution that may require permuting all matchings to identify the constrained welfare maximizing matching.
elements.sent-2411-061: They also analyze some conditions where positive strategy proofness results can be proven.
elements.sent-2411-062: We contribute through proving that, in general, there does not exist a constrained welfare maximizing and strategy proof mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-063: Additionally, we allow for indifference in preference rankings and constructively demonstrate that an analytically tractable and computationally feasible mechanism satisfying outcome sufficiency (Linear Exchange) exists.
elements.sent-2411-064: Our work also always allows for the match valuation to depend on the specific child-home match.
elements.sent-2411-065: Other works by \qcite{cechlarova-2017 and \qcite{saban-2015 explore the computational tractability of problems inherent in computing an outcome sufficient matching and motivate some of our restrictions on the problem (Assumption \ref{assumption:two-options ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: In the economics literature, \qcite{abdulkadiroglu-2021 and \qcite{bodoh-creed-2020 focus on finding welfare maximizing matchings in the context of school choice.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: School choice involves matching students to schools in a two-sided market where both sides are strategic and have preferences over the other side.
elements.sent-2411-068: The former work finds stability-constrained welfare maximizing matchings using a network-flow approach, and the latter work uses fractional approximation techniques from optimization to find welfare maximizing matchings that satisfy a modified definition of stability.
elements.sent-2411-069: Our contribution defines constrained welfare maximization in one-sided matching markets where the constraint is weak Pareto efficiency rather than stability, and our maximization requirement is more general than maximizing a sum of valuations.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is key in the context of child welfare as mechanisms that maximize sums can unfairly give weight to changing placements for children with large cardinal improvements in persistence.
elements.sent-2411-071: Lastly, a few select papers intersecting matching theory and child welfare loosely relate to our work.
elements.sent-2411-072: They show tangible benefits in increasing the number of placements.
elements.sent-2411-073: Some policies that focus on increasing market thickness, like matching across larger geographic regions, can significantly boost persistence.
elements.sent-2411-074: For a more comprehensive overview of the overlap between market design and child welfare systems, we refer the reader to this paper's sister \qcite{highsmith-2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: This work develops matching mechanisms for expedient adoptions from foster care where dynamic incentives play a far greater role than in matching children to temporary foster homes.
elements.sent-2411-076: Linear Exchange in Practice---Foster care in the United States is an interesting application of matching theory because, at present, there are no counties that we are aware of that utilize any formal matching mechanism to assign children to homes.
elements.sent-2411-077: One simple reason why this could be the case is that the status-quo, as we have described it, is equivalent to a weakly Pareto efficient matching mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-078: Therefore, all mechanisms developed in matching theory, save those that we described in our literature review, would have nearly or exactly the same efficiency as the status-quo\footnote{Serial Dictatorship appropriately modified for indifference could improve weak Pareto efficiency to Pareto efficiency.. .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Furthermore, two-sided matching mechanisms are not well-fit for foster care.
elements.sent-2411-080: This is because the set of children to be matched is fluid and constantly changing; the preferences that homes report, then, must similarly constantly update.
elements.sent-2411-081: One could construct preferences based on child observable characteristics which homes report once-and-for-all, but this would require additional theoretical developments to reach the same desirable properties as in the standard case.
elements.sent-2411-082: Additionally, as we will highlight as we present our model, evaluating a home is a costly task for caseworkers.
elements.sent-2411-083: Constructing long preferences lists is arduous on both sides of the market.
elements.sent-2411-084: Whether or not homes have preference ranking in foster care is also dubious; it is more likely that they have dichotomous preferences which allows the two-sided problem to be transformed into a one-sided problem.
elements.sent-2411-085: In contrast, Linear Exchange, an outcome sufficient mechanism, has the benefit that it maintains the status quo and improves persistence for all children.
elements.sent-2411-086: In working on this and related projects, we have conducted interviews with practitioners to understand the matching process in foster care.
elements.sent-2411-087: Our interviews indicate that individual caseworkers face a relatively easy problem: choose the best perceived home for a child.
elements.sent-2411-088: However, when practitioners aggregate this decision-making rule to the entire market, they find that many children end up unplaced when there is a reallocation that could guarantee more placements, and they find that persistence falls short of their goals.
elements.sent-2411-089: Both observations are consistent with the status quo which fails outcome sufficiency.
elements.sent-2411-090: One final concern with the implementation of a formal mechanism is that children often need to be matched quickly and frequently (for example, daily).
elements.sent-2411-091: In any static problem, only a handful of children might be involved in the mechanism, resulting in low market thickness.
elements.sent-2411-092: Outcome sufficiency reduces to weak Pareto efficiency when caseworkers' top choices for children are heterogeneous (which is more likely with low market thickness).
elements.sent-2411-093: Therefore, frequent matching might preclude gains from outcome sufficiency.
elements.sent-2411-094: We note that this would only be true in environments with frequent matching and few children in the foster care system.
elements.sent-2411-095: In large markets, there would still be a gain to outcome sufficiency.
elements.sent-2411-096: Furthermore, children within foster care, as noted, are often reassigned to new homes.
elements.sent-2411-097: Including these children within the mechanism increases market thickness and suggests that better outcome might follow.
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, utilizing Linear Exchange could provide rationale for aggregating matching decisions on coarser time scales such as weekly.
elements.sent-2411-099: Paper Organization---In Section 2, we detail our model preliminaries and mechanism properties.
elements.sent-2411-100: In Section 3, we give theoretical results on Linear Exchange, its non-strategic properties, and its strategic properties.
elements.sent-2411-101: We also present existence proofs for other mechanisms satisfying our properties.
elements.sent-2411-102: In Section 4, we detail our simulations and empirical results.
elements.sent-2411-103: Last, we conclude in Section 5 with practical considerations.

elements.sent-2411-162: Outcomes and Sufficiency in Child Welfare---In many practitioners' views, some features of foster care render it unamenable to algorithmic decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-163: Caseworkers routinely use qualitative information that is not easily encoded into usable data to make matching decisions.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our formulation would still allow caseworkers to declare preferences that utilize these qualitative datum, whereas previous work in this area has been abandoned because the algorithms in use frequently produced nonsensical and unusable results due to ignoring factors that caseworkers see as relevant.
elements.sent-2411-165: As it stands, to reap the boundless improvements from algorithmic decision-making seen in traditional matching theory applications like school choice, hospital-doctor matching, and kidney exchange, practitioners must agree to utilizing the methods invented in matching theory.
elements.sent-2411-166: Ensuring that they do not suffer more than the status quo is a binding constraint.
elements.sent-2411-167: Outcome sufficient matching guarantees this while allowing practitioners to implement tools from matching theory and predictive analytics.
elements.sent-2411-168: Concretely, a local matchmaker could use predicted placement stability as its outcome for children.
elements.sent-2411-169: Predicted placement stability is the probability that, if $c$ is placed at $h$, the placement would persist until $c$ permanently exits foster care.
elements.sent-2411-170: We will refer to this as persistence to avoid confusion with the matching theory term stability.
elements.sent-2411-171: Note that this outcome can only form a ranking over homes for a given child $c$.
elements.sent-2411-172: It cannot allow priority comparisons as is common in matching literature.
elements.sent-2411-173: To fix ideas, suppose that there are two families: $h$ and $h'$ as well as two children: $c$ and $c'$.
elements.sent-2411-174: A rudimentary solution to translate outcomes into priorities would be to assign priority at $h$ by ranking children's persistence at $h$.
elements.sent-2411-175: This would establish a priority order that disadvantages $c'$ at both homes, even though is seems intuitive that the matchmaker should assign $c$ to $h'$ and $c$ to $h$ whenever possible.
elements.sent-2411-176: Caseworkers in almost all counties in the United States effectively follow a decentralized approach where the earliest caseworker to find a suitable home for a child can contact that home to begin the match.
elements.sent-2411-177: This is equivalent to a serial dictatorship ordered by time; in the context of indifferences, this process will always yield a weakly Pareto efficient matching.
elements.sent-2411-178: We call this the status quo.
elements.sent-2411-179: Our work in this paper devises mechanisms that would select assignments that leave no room for improvement in persistence without compromising the status quo for caseworkers as weakly outcome sufficient mechanisms only yield assignments that are weakly Pareto efficient relative to their preferences.
elements.sent-2411-180: As we will detail in our empirical section, this simple strategy could greatly improve persistence in foster care and substantially benefit the tens of thousands of children entering foster care every year in the United States.
elements.sent-2411-181: Finally, implementing algorithmic matching could constitute a partial solution to the major labor supply issues seen in child welfare.
elements.sent-2411-182: Caseworkers are notoriously overwhelmed with large caseloads, and a simple system that uses an objective metric to place children with the best possible home could simplify caseworkers' decisions and decrease the amount of manual labor involved.
elements.sent-2411-183: Importantly, the algorithms we devise cannot replace caseworkers; we do not desire this.

elements.sent-2411-185: Thus, outcome sufficient matching would achieve the best of both worlds: maximum utilization of qualitative data present in caseworker preferences and automated optimization of child outcomes using predictive analytics.

elements.sent-2411-207: Results In this section, we detail our mechanism for computing outcome sufficient matchings and give important results on its properties.
elements.sent-2411-208: The mechanism we present here represents a new class of mechanisms which we call exchanges.
elements.sent-2411-209: The first distinct feature of an exchange is that a child may not be permanently matched after choosing in any given round, and her match may be subject to unilateral dissolution if another child is permitted to exchange for her home.
elements.sent-2411-210: The second distinct feature is the mirror of the first: once a child exits the exchange based on some rule, that child's match is permanent.
elements.sent-2411-211: The most important step is choosing a rule that guarantees weak Pareto efficiency without compromising weak outcome sufficiency.
elements.sent-2411-212: A (strict) Pareto improving cycle on $\mu$ is a cycle $(c_1, c_2, ..., c_n)$ where $\mu(c_{j+1 ) \succ_{c_j \mu(c_j)$ for all $j \neq n$ and $\mu(c_1) \succ_{c_n \mu(c_n)$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-223: With this proven, we can describe our mechanism for finding outcome sufficient matchings: Linear Exchange.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{mdframed} \begin{center} Algorithm 1. Linear Exchange ~\ \end{center} Initialize the following: the matching $\mu^1$ such that $\mu^1(c) = \varnothing$ for all $c$. The set of children initially in the exchange is $C^1 = C$ and $H^n = \{ h : \mu^n(h) = \varnothing \text{ or } \mu^n(h) \in C^n \}$. The most preferred set restricted to available, unmatched homes is $T^n(c) = \{ h : h \in T(c) \text{ and } \mu^n(h) = \varnothing \}$. ~\ Round $n \geq 1$: According to an arbitrary order, select the next $c \in C^n$ with $\mu^n(c) = \varnothing$. Let $h^* = m^*(c|H^n)$. \begin{itemize} \item $h^* \in T(c)$: then set $\mu^{n+1}(\mu^n(h^*)) = \varnothing$, $\mu^{n+1}(c) = h^*$, and $C^{n+1} = C^n - c$. \item $h^* \notin T(c)$: Suppose that $T^n(c) = \varnothing$. $h \in H^n$ is a no-trade home for $c$ if $h \in H^n$ and there is no strictly Pareto improving cycle among children not in the exchange when $c$ matches to $h$. The set of no-trade homes for $c$ is $NT^n(c)$. If $h^{NT} = m^*(c|NT^n(c))$ is defined, then set $\mu^{n+1}(\mu^n(h^{NT})) = \varnothing$ and $\mu^{n+1}(c) = h^{NT}$. Set $C^{n+1} = C^n - c$. If $T^n(c) \neq \varnothing$, then set $\mu^{n+1}(c) = m^*(c|T^n(c))$ and $C^{n+1} = C^n$. \end{itemize} If $C^{n+1}$ or $H^{n+1}$ are empty or the end of the exchange is reached, terminate and return $\mu^{n+1}$. Otherwise, continue to round $n+1$. \end{mdframed}
elements.sent-2411-227: In every round, either a child is matched or a child is unmatched while another is matched and permanently removed from the exchange.
elements.sent-2411-228: The structure of an exchange mechanism is similar to dictatorial mechanisms in that an arbitrary ordering determines a series of children that will, in turn, select their most preferable option from all available options.
elements.sent-2411-229: However, it differs in the key feature that children with potential to improve their outcomes remain in the exchange, and children that would Pareto- and outcome-improve their matching by replacing a children within the exchange can do so.
elements.sent-2411-230: The surprise of the Linear Exchange mechanism is that, when the replacement rule is carefully designed not to generate strict Pareto cycles among children outside of the exchange, replacements will not generate beneficial trades among any children nor will they leave potential outcome improvement cycles unrealized.
elements.sent-2411-231: The sole exception to the latter point is that there might exist a matching that reallocates children remaining in the exchange at termination and outcome dominates $\phi_{LE}$.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, since these children never exited the exchange, they must all be matched to their most preferred homes.
elements.sent-2411-233: Any matching that outcome dominates through reallocating these children must assign at least one to a least preferred option to improve that child's outcome.
elements.sent-2411-234: Thus, this reallocation will cause the matching to be Pareto dominated by $\phi_{LE}$.
elements.sent-2411-235: This result is a significant step in finding more general conditions under which Pareto constrained welfare maximization in matching problems is computationally feasible.

elements.sent-2411-319: Empirical Performance In this section, we provide the technical details for our simulations and show that Linear Exchange substantially increases welfare in the markets that we examine.
elements.sent-2411-320: For our simulations, we generate environments that we expect to mimic child assignment to homes.
elements.sent-2411-321: We split our simulations into a small market case with five to ten children and a large market case with twenty to fifty children.
elements.sent-2411-322: Both are representative of a typical foster care matching market in small and large counties, respectively\footnote{\qcite{cortes-2021 documents that Los Angeles county had about forty placements per day between January and February 2011.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-323: The number of homes in a market is uniformly randomly distributed according to the same values\footnote{Systemic market imbalances do not affect our results.}
elements.sent-2411-324: We can easily capture capacitated homes by cloning homes and carefully breaking ties in preferred outcomes; we leave this for future robustness checks.. .
elements.sent-2411-325: We simulate preferences and outcomes under five different specifications.
elements.sent-2411-326: The first specification is uncorrelated, unaligned preferences and outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-327: Concretely, for each child $c$, $T(c)$ is a uniformly random subset of elements from $H$ with $|T(c)| = N(T(c))$.
elements.sent-2411-328: After the realization of $T(c)$ is drawn, we draw $B(c)$ which is a uniformly random subset of elements from $H - T(c)$.
elements.sent-2411-329: Its size is a random variable $N(B(c))$ uniformly distributed over integers in $[1, |H| - |T(c)|]$.
elements.sent-2411-330: The preferred outcomes for $c$ at a home $h$ is determined by $c$'s predicted persistence at $h$ which is a random variable $Y_c(h) \sim U[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-331: Our second specification is child-correlated preferences and unaligned outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-332: In this specification, there is a random set of common most preferred homes $T$.
elements.sent-2411-333: We define $T(c)$ as above then union this set with $T$ to obtain "correlated" preferences.
elements.sent-2411-334: This procedure suffices to communicate our message that conflicting most preferred homes allows Linear Exchange to optimally reallocate homes while maintaining weak Pareto efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-335: Our third, fourth, and fifth specifications involve uncorrelated preferences and aligned outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-336: In this model, $\alpha \in \{ 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 \}$ measures the degree of alignment between child preferences and preferred outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-337: Every $c$ has at least $\alpha*H$, rounded, of the matchmaker's top outcomes for her in $T(c)$.
elements.sent-2411-338: We exclude $\alpha = 1$ because this trivializes the problem.
elements.sent-2411-339: Linear Exchange would always select the unconstrained welfare maximizing matching.
elements.sent-2411-340: We use these specifications to capture the fact that the wedge between the status-quo and outcome sufficiency may be smaller when, in some sense, children and the matchmaker agree on the most preferred home for a child.
elements.sent-2411-341: In every specification, we run 1000 simulations and compare the status quo, Serial Dictatorship with randomly broken ties (see the Appendix for details), and Linear Exchange.
elements.sent-2411-342: We focus on the persistence rate averaged across children and simulations under the two mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-343: In the future after we have our experimental results, we can follow a similar process using elicited preferences rather than simulated preferences.
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Persistence, Uncorrelated vs. Correlated} \begin{tabular}{l@{ 6ex}c} Specification Base Corr Average persistence, small market Linear Exchange 71.6\% 75.2\% Serial Dictatorship 50\% 49.8\% Average persistence, large market\rule{0pt}{6ex} Linear Exchange 86.5\% 89.8\% Serial Dictatorship 50\% 49.9\% \end{tabular} \label{table:small-market-corr}
elements.sent-2411-344: Small Markets---Despite our initial concerns about Linear Exchange lacking welfare improvements in small markets that we detailed in the Introduction, Linear Exchange performs very well.
elements.sent-2411-345: In the base case, Linear Exchange improves persistence by 21.6 pp. When preferences are correlated across children, Linear Exchange improves by an additional 3.8 pp as it can exploit conflicts over most preferred homes to promote better outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-346: The same patterns hold under aligned preferences, where Linear Exchange continues to outpace the status quo by at least 20 pp in every specification.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Persistence, Unaligned vs. Aligned} \begin{tabular}{l@{ 6ex}c@{ 6ex}c} Specification Base $0.25$ $0.5$ $0.75$ Average persistence, small market Linear Exchange 71.6\% 78.2\% 78.5\% 78.5\% Serial Dictatorship 50\% 56.5\% 56.5\% 55\% Average persistence, large market\rule{0pt}{6ex} Linear Exchange 86.5\% 92.4\% 92.5\% 92.3\% Serial Dictatorship 50\% 59.6\% 58.7\% 55.2\% \end{tabular} \label{table:small-market-align}
elements.sent-2411-347: Large Markets---On the baseline, Linear Exchange is more welfare enhancing---36.5 pp over the status quo---in large markets compared to small markets.
elements.sent-2411-348: The rationale behind this is exactly as we explained in the Introduction.
elements.sent-2411-349: Large markets have greater market thickness leading to better placement options for children on average and a larger space of weakly Pareto efficient matchings.
elements.sent-2411-350: Linear Exchange selects the outcome sufficient matching from this larger space which mechanically improves outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-352: Linear Exchange gains a marginal 3.4 pp under correlated preferences.
elements.sent-2411-353: In every specification with aligned preferences, its improvement is over 22 pp compared to the status quo.
elements.sent-2411-354: In either small or large markets, increasing $\alpha$ beyond 0.25 does not substantially change persistence rates.
elements.sent-2411-355: This is most likely owing to the fact that there is not sufficient correlation in preferences to induce conflicts in most preferred homes among children.
elements.sent-2411-356: If the matchmaker's top preferred outcome aligns with a child's most preferred home, there may be no other child whom would want the first child's most preferred-outcome home.
elements.sent-2411-357: Therefore, the only relevant degree of alignment will be that the matchmaker's most preferred outcome should be one of the child's most preferred homes.
elements.sent-2411-358: Under Serial Dictatorship, increasing alignment fails to bridge the gap between it and Linear Exchange.
elements.sent-2411-359: This result flows from the fact that, when the rule to break indifference does not depend on the matchmaker's preferred outcomes (as is often the case in practice where search costs might imply that the first most preferred home that a caseworker finds is the one that she selects), increasing alignment is only helpful if the aligned homes are the only homes that the child has as her most preferred homes.
elements.sent-2411-360: Conclusion Before concluding, we highlight a number of salient future directions for our simulations.
elements.sent-2411-361: As mentioned in the Introduction, our tentative plan involves running a lab-in-the-field experiment with real caseworkers to evaluate their preferences and decision-making in a simulated market where children's predicted persistence is determined through a measure validated in a real U.S.

elements.sent-2411-367: We use a new property, outcome sufficiency, to characterize mechanisms that maintain the status quo while improving outcomes for all children.
elements.sent-2411-368: Our mechanism, Linear Exchange, satisfies outcome sufficiency, though it (and all outcome sufficient mechanisms) fails strategy proofness.
elements.sent-2411-369: Nevertheless, it has some robustness against strategic manipulations as it is non-obviously manipulable.

elements.sent-2411-371: As a final note, while our application focuses on child welfare, outcome sufficient mechanisms can be applied to many other one-sided matching markets where welfare maximization is a goal in tandem with efficiency requirements.

elements.sent-2411-497: Introduction The traditional goal of matching theory is to use agents' preferences to achieve an assignment of objects to agents or agents to agents that is efficient, fair, and sustainable, or some combination of all three.
elements.sent-2411-498: However, in some organizational contexts, matching based on individual decision-maker preferences can hamper an institution's goals.
elements.sent-2411-499: Decision-makers, even when attempting to pursue the organizational objective, can fall prey to informational asymmetries, cognitive limitations, or a lack of experience.
elements.sent-2411-500: Simultaneously, even if the organization has access to an algorithm that can outperform the decision-makers, it may be infeasible to implement in practice due to legal, ethical, and trust concerns.
elements.sent-2411-501: In this paper, we develop one-sided matching mechanisms that input two preference rankings.
elements.sent-2411-502: We take it as given that there is some underlying welfare attained when any decision-maker matches to any object.
elements.sent-2411-503: We represent decision-maker (DM) preferences over objects, which might or might not align with welfare, as one set of preference rankings.
elements.sent-2411-504: The second set of preference rankings---which we call evaluations---are the ordinal representation of an algorithm's predictions of welfare between DM-object matches.
elements.sent-2411-505: We design mechanisms that satisfy a fairness notion that we call unanimity, two weaker efficiency properties for unanimous matching mechanisms, and non-obvious manipulability under some conditions.
elements.sent-2411-506: Following this, we apply our mechanisms to the child welfare domain.
elements.sent-2411-507: In the United States, the foster care system is responsible for caring for children found to be victims of abuse or neglect at the hands of their parents.
elements.sent-2411-508: Generally speaking, children that are suspected to be victims of abuse or neglect are often reported to the local child welfare agency by community members.
elements.sent-2411-509: The agency assigns a caseworker (DM) to represent the child, and the child can be kept in custody of the county for up to several days before she is matched to a temporary foster home (object).
elements.sent-2411-510: On the contrary, some caseworkers might have erroneous judgment causing them to systemically choose homes where children will not persist.
elements.sent-2411-511: One objective goal in the matching process is placement stability (\qcitenp{carnochan-2013 ).}
elements.sent-2411-512: We model this problem as a one-sided matching market where children are strategic agents allocated to homes (objects)\footnote{In practice, homes can have dichotomous preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-513: However, this two-sided matching problem reduces to a one-sided matching problem where a child finds a home acceptable only if the child and home find the match acceptable.. .
elements.sent-2411-514: Children have strict ordinal preferences $\succ_c$ over homes\footnote{As aforementioned, caseworkers are the DMs and actually hold preferences, but we refer to them as children in exposition for simplicity.}
elements.sent-2411-515: One can think of the caseworkers as analogous to parents in school choice.. .
elements.sent-2411-516: The matchmaker has strict ordinal preferences $>_c$ over homes that a given child can be placed in.
elements.sent-2411-518: We introduce a fairness concept termed unanimity.
elements.sent-2411-519: A home $h$ is unanimously acceptable for $c$ if $h \succ_c \varnothing$ and $h >_c \varnothing$.
elements.sent-2411-520: A child $c$ has a unanimous improvement over $h$ if there exists another $h'$ where $h' \succ_c h$ and $h' >_c h$.
elements.sent-2411-521: A home is unanimous for a child if the child has no unanimous improvement over it.
elements.sent-2411-522: A matching $\mu$ is unanimous if there is no other matching which can weakly expand the set of children matched to unanimous homes and the set of children matched to unanimously acceptable homes.
elements.sent-2411-523: Unanimity is a form of preference aggregation, and our particular method of aggregation prioritizes allocations where the matchmaker eliminates or reduces matches where a DM and the algorithm would jointly agree that another match is superior.
elements.sent-2411-524: This desideratum is fair under the prior that these strong agreements between humans and algorithms should be respected over simply improving preferences or evaluations alone.
elements.sent-2411-525: Simultaneously, unanimity provides robustness in cases where one decision model--the human or the algorithm---is fundamentally flawed but the other is more accurate in predicting welfare.
elements.sent-2411-526: Unanimity is useful when institutional constraints prohibit fully automating the matching process and require respecting human preferences as is the case in foster care.
elements.sent-2411-527: We show a simple equivalence between unanimity and an efficient matching with appropriately aggregated preferences (Proposition \ref{proposition:unanimous-reduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-528: The equivalence shows that the Serial Dictatorship (SD) mechanism appropriately modified for indifference (Serial Dictatorship with Indifference or SDI) generates unanimous matchings (Proposition \ref{proposition:sdi-unanimous ).}
elements.sent-2411-529: Many matchings can be unanimous, and a unanimous and efficient matching can fail to exist.
elements.sent-2411-530: Thus, we impose further efficiency restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-531: We tweak the Top Trading Cycles mechanism to find Pareto improvements on any unanimous matching while maintaining unanimity.
elements.sent-2411-532: Next, we innovate the concept of a Serial Dictatorship to allow for selective replacements in matches to objects and show that our mechanism, Linear Exchange (LE), achieves a median between unanimity and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-533: Its matchings are more unanimous than a large class of matchings and more efficient (or incomparably efficient) than any unanimous matching (Theorem \ref{theorem:linear_exchange_positive ) Turning to strategic incentives, we show that no unanimous mechanism can be strategy proof (Proposition \ref{proposition:unanimous-sp ).}}
elements.sent-2411-534: Worse, we show that every unanimous mechanism is obviously manipulable (Theorem \ref{theorem:obvious-manipulability ).}
elements.sent-2411-536: Nevertheless, we restore some positive results regarding the strategic incentives of SDI and LE.
elements.sent-2411-537: When the number of homes is smaller than the number of children, as is typically the case in foster care, both mechanisms are non-obviously manipulable.
elements.sent-2411-538: We also discuss the fact that children may generally only desire unanimous improvements when they recognize that the algorithm can be complementary to welfare.
elements.sent-2411-539: In this case, no harmful manipulations will occur.
elements.sent-2411-540: Our results suggest that we might be able to attain a limited version of strategy-proofness that we term group robustness.
elements.sent-2411-541: Group robustness requires that there is no profitable manipulation that can improve all children with respect to either $\succ$ or $>$.
elements.sent-2411-542: This concept is important because the matchings we select may not be efficient with respect to either order which can afford opportunities for children or the matchmaker to improve without harming anyone.
elements.sent-2411-543: We generalize our model to examine whether or not any mechanism and aggregated preferences can satisfy group robustness.
elements.sent-2411-544: Unfortunately, we find a negative result: any strategy-proof and efficient mechanism satisfying mild regularity conditions that uses aggregated preferences cannot be group robust unless the aggregation is equivalent to $\succ$ or $>$ (Theorem \ref{theorem:gs-aggregation ).}

elements.sent-2411-556: Caseworkers' preferences do not align with predicted persistence, and these results suggest that they do not align with actual persistence.
elements.sent-2411-557: Moreover, we are able to demonstrate that there are match-specific benefits to persistence, a fact not observed in previous work.
elements.sent-2411-558: In the current version of the paper, we include a fully simulated empirical section.
elements.sent-2411-559: We show that across many specifications where a predictive algorithm may be inaccurate and even highly inaccurate when caseworkers are accurate, utilizing SDI, UTTC, or LE improves average persistence by 6.5\% at worst and 28.3\% at best.
elements.sent-2411-560: Literature Contribution---As far as we are aware, no other literature on one-sided matching has ever considered the problem where two rankings may be involved.
elements.sent-2411-561: However, in the adjacent literature on social choice theory, many such issues involving preference aggregation arise.
elements.sent-2411-562: We see our work as a near direct extension of \qcite{danan-2016 to matching theory.}
elements.sent-2411-563: Their unambiguous Pareto dominance property emphasizes choosing acts where agents unambiguously agree in the face of ex-ante uncertainty about what social outcomes the acts entail.
elements.sent-2411-564: Conceptually, unanimity is quite similar to this property.
elements.sent-2411-565: The main difference lies in the fact that unanimity is appropriate for application to matching allocations rather than a society's preferences.
elements.sent-2411-566: Further, we remain agnostic as to the formation of the rankings whereas the aforementioned work uses assumptions on preferences to derive important main results.
elements.sent-2411-567: Furthermore, we integrate preference aggregation with matching theory, showing that unanimity is equivalent to efficiency with respect to a preference aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-568: Our work then extends \qcite{gibbard-1973 to our setting showing that there is little hope of preempting strategic incentives.}
elements.sent-2411-569: Additionally, this paper intersects results on Pareto-constrained welfare maximization in the computer science literature.
elements.sent-2411-570: Our evaluation ranking can be reformulated as a cardinal match valuation.

Linear Exchange \end{center} Initialize the following: the matching $\mu^1$ such that $\mu^1(c) = \varnothing$ for all $c$. The set of children initially in the exchange is $C^1 = C$ and $H^n = \{ h : \mu^n(h) = \varnothing \text{ or } \mu^n(h) \in C^n \}$. $c$'s unanimous homes restricted to available, unmatched homes are $H^*_n(c) = \{ h : h \in H^*(c) \text{ and } \mu^n(h) = \varnothing \}$. Round $n \geq 1$: According to an arbitrary order, select the next $c \in C^n$ with $\mu^n(c) = \varnothing$. Let $h^*$ be $c$'s most preferred, unanimously acceptable home in $H^n$. \begin{itemize} \item $h^* \in H^*(c)$: then set $\mu^{n+1}(\mu^n(h^*)) = \varnothing$, $\mu^{n+1}(c) = h^*$, and $C^{n+1} = C^n - c$. \item $h^* \notin H^*(c)$: Suppose that $H^*_n(c) = \varnothing$. $h \in H^n$ is a no-trade home for $c$ if $h \in H^n$ and there is no SU-cycle among children not in the exchange when $c$ matches to $h$. The set of no-trade homes for $c$ is $NT^n(c)$. If the set is not empty, let $h^{NT}$ be $c$'s most preferred home in $NT^n(c)$. Set $\mu^{n+1}(\mu^n(h^{NT})) = \varnothing$ and $\mu^{n+1}(c) = h^{NT}$. Set $C^{n+1} = C^n - c$. If $H^*_n(c) \neq \varnothing$, then set $\mu^{n+1}(c)$ to $c$'s most preferred home in $H^*_n(c)$ and $C^{n+1} = C^n$. \end{itemize} If $C^{n+1}$ or $H^{n+1}$ are empty or the end of the exchange is reached, terminate and return $\mu^{n+1}$. Otherwise, continue to round $n+1$. \end{singleframedindent}

elements.sent-2411-1107: In every specification, we run one-thousand simulations and compare the status quo, Serial Dictatorship, with SDI, UTTC, and LE.

Adaptive Serial Dictatorship with Indifference \end{center} Initialize the following: the set of chosen matchings $U^n$ such that $U^1 = U$ and remaining homes $H^1 = H$. Round $n \geq 1$: According to an arbitrary order, select the next child $c$ that has not been a dictator. Subroutine A Construct the unanimous ranking for $c$ over remaining homes $H^n$. Denote the round $n$ unanimous ranking for $c$ as $>_{I(c)}^n$. Subroutine B Set $U_c = \{ \mu \in U^n : \mu(c) \geq^n_{I(c)} \mu'(c) \text{ for all } \mu' \in U^n \text{ and } \mu(c) >^n_{I(c)} \varnothing\}$. Set $U^{n+1} = U_c$ and $H^{n+1} = \{h : \mu(h) = \varnothing \text{ for some } \mu \in U^{n+1}\}$ If all children have been dictators or $H^{n+1} = \varnothing$, then terminate and return any $\mu \in U^{n+1}$. Otherwise, continue to round $n+1$. \end{singleframedindent}

elements.sent-2411-1417: Experiment Design In this section, we assess the welfare implications of our mechanisms using experimental analysis.
elements.sent-2411-1418: We detail our choice experiment design to elicit real caseworker preferences in a simulated market.
elements.sent-2411-1419: Our simple experimental mechanism elicits subjects' ordinal preferences in an "elicited preferences" framework.
elements.sent-2411-1420: We partition subjects into small markets with five children and five homes each, and we privately randomize each home to be either available or unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-1421: Each subject receives a unique child assignment detailing her child's characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-1422: The subjects then rank five homes.
elements.sent-2411-1423: Our mechanism then matches each subject's child to her favorite available home.
elements.sent-2411-1424: Subjects are incentivized to match their children to homes where the subject evaluates that the child would persist.
elements.sent-2411-1425: The procedure is incentive-compatible and yields subjects' true preferences for the task of matching children to homes to maximize persistence.
elements.sent-2411-1426: Our experiments' subjects are real caseworkers recruited through the research arm of a organization promoting foster care, allowing us to evaluate practitioners' behavior.
elements.sent-2411-1427: In what follows, we provide the economic environment, preference parameters of interest, and experimental procedures.
elements.sent-2411-1428: The experiment has not yet been run, so we do not present results.

elements.sent-2411-1431: We do not specify caseworker preferences because our goal is to elicit their true preferences for a matching objective which we will specify shortly.
elements.sent-2411-1432: We allow any matching between any child and any home in a market to be admissible.
elements.sent-2411-1433: The true persistence for a child $c$ to a home $h$ is an unobserved outcome $Y(c,h) = 1$ if $c$ would persist at $h$ and $Y(c,h) = 0$ if not.

elements.sent-2411-1527: Random Forest Predictor---Random forests are a machine learning method for classification and regression analysis.
elements.sent-2411-1528: They are collections of decision trees that partition data based on features (covariates) to minimize a loss function in predicting some outcome.
elements.sent-2411-1529: Random forests use bootstrap aggregating to draw a random subsample from the data and train a decision tree on the bootstrap sample using a random selection of features.
elements.sent-2411-1530: The forest predicts a classification outcome through majority vote of the ensemble of decision trees.
elements.sent-2411-1531: Random forests are known to be highly accurate and minimize variance compared to many competing machine learning methods, inspiring our use of this method to predict persistence.
elements.sent-2411-1532: We refer the reader to \qcite{breiman-2001 for details on training a random forest and utilizing it for prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-1533: We train the random forest to predict persistence on a random subsample using 75\% of the placements.
elements.sent-2411-1534: We tune our parameters for the random forest to use one-hundred trees, maximum depth and splitting, a Gini loss measure, and a maximum of square root of the total number of features.
elements.sent-2411-1535: We use all features in the Placement Files for our training and display their importances in Figure \ref{fig:feature-importances .}
elements.sent-2411-1536: After training the random forest, we assess its validity in the remaining 25\% test sample.
elements.sent-2411-1537: The accuracy in our test sample is a remarkable 77\%.
elements.sent-2411-1538: We report the confusion matrix in Figure \ref{fig:confusion-matrix .}
elements.sent-2411-1539: The false negative rate is approximately 15\%, and the false positive rate is approximately 7.8\%.

elements.sent-2411-1571: Experiment Procedure Before conducting the experiment, we performed simulations and power analyses to assess the optimal sample size.

elements.sent-2411-1573: We determine that a minimum of three-hundred subjects would be ideal to detect effects in our experiment.
elements.sent-2411-1574: We recruit caseworkers through child welfare agencies in counties across the United States.
elements.sent-2411-1575: We reached out to these counties to disseminate an email invitation to our experiment sent to caseworker employees.
elements.sent-2411-1576: Subjects that read and responded to our email were able to immediately participate in the online experiment.
elements.sent-2411-1577: The experiment was available from DATE to DATE.
elements.sent-2411-1578: We allowed subjects to asynchronously complete the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-1579: The experiment begins with an instructions interface that explains relevant terminology, the economic environment, and rules.
elements.sent-2411-1580: While or after reading the instructions, the subject fills out (incentivized) control questions that verifies her understanding.
elements.sent-2411-1581: Once complete, we ask subjects to answer a basic demographics survey that includes an additional question soliciting the subject's experience as a foster care caseworker.
elements.sent-2411-1582: After finishing the instructions and survey, the subject proceeds to the experimental interface to complete the five rounds of the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-1583: Subjects were allotted ten minutes per round to read their child profile, home profiles, and submit preferences.
elements.sent-2411-1584: The average time to complete a round was TIME.
elements.sent-2411-1585: Once a subject completed her rounds, she receives her payoff and exits the experiment immediately.
elements.sent-2411-1586: We pay subjects a baseline participation fee of ten dollars USD plus her gains or loses from incentive pay.
elements.sent-2411-1587: The conversion rate for one experimental unit was ten-cents USD.
